---
title: Reference template
description: A lookup-oriented layout for APIs, configuration keys, commands, and syntax.
---

Use this layout when readers need facts on demand — not a story or a walkthrough. Reference pages are for scanning, copying exact syntax, and confirming allowed values.

<Info>
  Examples on the **Templates** pages share a fictional **Coffee Shop Ordering API** so you can compare how the same product reads across documentation types.
</Info>

## Blueprint anatomy

Before writing, anchor yourself to the core constraints of the information-oriented quadrant:

- **The goal:** Provide a strict, lookup-oriented directory of facts — API specs, configuration keys, command syntax, or component props.
- **The tone:** Stark, literal, objective, and dense. No welcome paragraphs, analogies, or marketing language.
- **The layout:** Syntax → Parameters or options → Examples → Errors → See also. Use `<Property>` and `<Accordion>` for nested fields; use tables for flat lists.

| Use reference when… | Use something else when… |
| --- | --- |
| The reader knows *what* they need to look up | They need a first success path → [Tutorial](/templates/tutorial) |
| Fields, types, defaults, and errors must be exhaustive | They need steps for one task → [How-to](/templates/how-to) |
| Prose should stay minimal | They need architecture and tradeoffs → [Explanation](/templates/explanation) |

## Copy this template

Copy this raw skeleton into a new `.mdx` file to begin scaffolding your page.

````mdx title="docs/reference/your-surface.mdx"
---
title: [Resource or endpoint name]
description: [What this page documents — parameters, errors, or syntax]
---

[One-line austere description of the surface]

## Syntax

```http
[METHOD] [path or command format]
```

## [Parameters / Options / Fields]

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `[field]` | `[type]` | [Yes/No] | [Description and allowed values] |

## Example request

```json
[Valid request or config snippet]
```

## Example response

```json
[Valid response or output]
```

## Errors

| Status / condition | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| `[code or condition]` | [When this occurs] |

## See also

- [Link to Tutorial]
- [Link to How-to]
- [Link to Explanation]
````

## Example: Create order (`POST /v1/orders`)

Below is a live preview of how a finished reference page reads. Notice the absence of onboarding prose and the emphasis on allowed values.

<Card title="Live preview">

#### Syntax

```http
POST /v1/orders
Authorization: Bearer <api_key>
Content-Type: application/json
```

#### Request parameters

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `item` | `string` | Yes | Base beverage. Allowed values: `espresso`, `americano`, `latte`, `cappuccino`. |
| `size` | `string` | Yes | Drink size. Allowed values: `single`, `double`, `twelve_oz`, `sixteen_oz`. |
| `temperature` | `string` | No | Defaults to `hot`. Allowed values: `hot`, `iced`. |

#### Example request

```json
{
  "item": "latte",
  "size": "twelve_oz",
  "temperature": "iced"
}
```

#### Example response

```json
{
  "order_id": "ord_123",
  "status": "received",
  "total_cents": 525
}
```

#### Errors

| Status | Condition |
| --- | --- |
| `401 Unauthorized` | Missing or invalid API key. |
| `422 Unprocessable Entity` | Invalid combination — for example, `single` size with an `iced` latte. |

</Card>

## How reference treats code

In reference, code blocks are **data definitions** — request shapes, config snippets, and response samples. Do not embed numbered procedures; link to [Tutorial](/templates/tutorial) or [How-to](/templates/how-to) instead.
